What ADC resolution does the ESP32-S3 have, and which pins can I use as ADC inputs?

ESP32-S3 has two SAR ADCs, each 12-bit, range 0-3.3 V (with default attenuation 2.5 dB it's 0-1.1 V; for 0-3.3 V set 11 dB). ADC1 channels are GPIO 1-10; ADC2 channels are GPIO 11-20. Caveat: ADC2 cannot be used while WiFi is active because the WiFi driver borrows it. Stick to ADC1 (GPIO 1-10) for any reading you do alongside WiFi/BLE. The chip's INL is around +/-12 LSB; for serious accuracy calibrate against a known reference and oversample 16x.

What's the max GPIO sink current on an Arduino Uno R4?

Per pin: 8 mA continuous, 20 mA absolute max (RA4M1 datasheet). Per port: 80 mA. Don't drive a relay coil directly (typical 70 mA); use a transistor or MOSFET. The R4 uses 5 V tolerant 3.3 V GPIO at the MCU level; the on-board level shifters bring the headers to 5 V. Direct drive: a single LED through a current-limiting resistor is fine. Multiple LEDs on the same port: stay under 80 mA combined. The on-board LED on D13 has its own driver and is fine.

Which Pico GPIOs can do PWM, and how many independent frequencies can I run?

All 30 GPIOs on the RP2040 can do PWM via the PWM peripheral. The chip has 8 PWM 'slices', each with two channels (A and B), giving 16 channels total. Each slice has its own frequency / period / divider; channels A and B share frequency but have independent duty. So you can run 8 different frequencies simultaneously, with up to 2 different duty cycles per frequency. Pin to slice mapping: GPIO 0 -> 0A, 1 -> 0B, 2 -> 1A, 3 -> 1B, ..., GPIO 28 -> 6A. PIO can do PWM too (uses a state machine instead of the dedicated peripheral) for arbitrary patterns.

Does the Adafruit Feather nRF52840 Sense have a built-in IMU?

Yes, the LSM6DS33 (6-axis accelerometer + gyroscope) is on board, on the secondary I2C bus. It's also got the LIS3MDL magnetometer (3-axis, separate I2C address), the SHT3x temperature/humidity, the BMP280 pressure, the APDS-9960 gesture/proximity, and a PDM microphone. All the sensors hang off the internal Wire bus (SDA P0.07 / SCL P0.08 in nRF terms; in Arduino it's just `Wire`). Power: SPI flash and sensors are gated through a 3.3 V load switch you can disable for ultra-low-power sleep.

Why does the Seeed Xiao ESP32-C3 have only 4 ADC pins?

The ESP32-C3 chip itself has 6 ADC channels but the Xiao C3 only breaks out 4 (A0-A3 = GPIO 2/3/4/5) because of its small form factor. The other two ADC inputs are tied to the on-board USB-D+/D- routing or the boot strap pins. ADC is 12-bit, single SAR (no ADC2 vs ADC1 split like on bigger ESP32). Maximum input voltage with default attenuation is 1.1 V; with 11 dB it's 3.3 V. Same WiFi caveat does not apply because there's only one ADC, but the ADC has noticeable noise (~3 mV) and a small offset; calibrate.

Arduino Nano 33 BLE has no analogWrite() on most pins. What gives?

The Nano 33 BLE uses the nRF52840 (mbed core), not an AVR. PWM on mbed-Arduino is implemented via four PWM modules with three channels each, giving 12 PWM-capable pins. The first call to `analogWrite(pin, x)` on a pin allocates a PWM channel; once 12 are in use, further calls do nothing. Pins that are not PWM-allocated already include D2, D3, D4, D5, D6, D9, D10, A0, A1, A2, A3, A4, A5. If `analogWrite` does nothing, you've hit the 12-channel limit; release a previous pin or use the mbed Pwm API directly. Default frequency is 500 Hz; change it with `analogWriteResolution()` and the mbed_pwm library.

Pico W vs Pico: what's actually different?

Pico W adds the Infineon CYW43439 chip for 2.4 GHz WiFi (b/g/n) and Bluetooth 5.2 (LE only, no Classic). The CYW43 sits on its own SPI to the RP2040, sharing pins with VBUS sensing and the on-board LED. As a result on the Pico W: the on-board LED is GPIO 'CYW43_LED' (a virtual pin via the WiFi chip's GPIO 0), NOT GP25 like on the Pico. ADC channels VSYS sense and the temperature sensor are at slightly different pins. WiFi adds 80-120 mA when associated, 12 mA in PS-poll, 1.6 mA in deep sleep with WiFi off. Cost difference is $2.

What's special about pin 7 on the Adafruit Feather ESP32-S3?

GPIO 7 on the Feather ESP32-S3 is wired to the orange LED for boot status. It's not exclusive: you can use it as a regular GPIO if you don't mind blinking the LED with your code. Some Feather variants use GPIO 13 instead; check the silkscreen on your specific Feather. The chip has 45 GPIOs but the Feather breaks out 21 of them; the ones not broken out drive USB, the boot button, the LED, and the on-board PSRAM.

Uno R4 WiFi: what 14-bit hardware does it have that Uno R3 doesn't?

RA4M1 MCU on the R4 brings: a 14-bit DAC on A0 (one channel, 12-bit usable in Arduino API by default; bump with `analogWriteResolution(14)`), 12-bit ADC on A0-A5, hardware Serial1 on D0/D1 plus an on-board ESP32-S3 acting as a WiFi co-processor over a separate UART. The R3 only had 10-bit ADC and no DAC. The R4 also has a real 12x8 LED matrix on board (40 LEDs in a charlieplex grid, programmable as a low-res text/icon scroller).

ESP32-S3 GPIO 19 / 20 pull-ups: what's the gotcha?

GPIO 19 and 20 are also USB D- and D+. On the bare chip the USB peripheral grabs them whenever USB is enumerated; if you try to use them as regular GPIO while USB is active, both peripherals fight. On the DevKitC-1, they're routed to the USB connector. Either don't use these pins for general I/O, or build firmware without USB-CDC enabled and use UART for serial. Boot strap pins also matter on S3: GPIO 0, 3, 45, 46 are sampled at reset; do not pull them strongly during boot.

Nano 33 BLE has both an MbedOS core and a stock Arduino core. Which should I use for CONDUYT firmware?

Stick with the Mbed-based Arduino Nano 33 BLE core (the official ArduinoBLE-supporting one). The CONDUYT BLE transport layer relies on ArduinoBLE, which targets that core. The community 'ArduinoCore-mbed' has both, but the ArduinoBLE library is built against mbed only. PlatformIO config: `platform = nordicnrf52`, `board = nano33ble`, `framework = arduino`. ATM the Mbed OS layer adds ~30 KB overhead but you get clean BLE; the ZephyrRTOS-based experimental cores are not yet stable for production CONDUYT.
